replies.text:
@GeraltofRivia22 yeah the New Shadow, as Tolkien wrote in letter:

"I did begin a story placed about 100 years after the Downfall [of Sauron], but it proved both sinister and depressing. Since we are dealing with Men it is inevitable that we should be concerned with the most regrettable feature of their nature: their quick satiety with good. So that the people of Gondor in times of peace, justice and prosperity, would become discontented and restless - while the dynasts descended from Aragorn would become just kings and governors - like Denethor or worse. I found that even so early there was an outcrop of revolutionary plots, about a centre of secret Satanistic religion; while Gondorian boys were playing at being Orcs and going round doing damage. I could have written a 'thriller' about the plot and its discovery and overthrow - but it would be just that. Not worth doing."

But hell even if he did eventually write it and it was just a thriller fantasy...it would be no doubt a remarkable tale in it's own right....still he was wise enough to know when to stop!

Tolkien knew human nature...

"I have written nothing beyond the first few years of the Fourth Age. (Except the beginning of a tale supposed to refer to the end of the reign of Eldarion about 100 years after the death of Aragorn. Then I of course discovered that the King's Peace would contain no tales worth recounting; and his wars would have little interest after the overthrow of Sauron; but that almost certainly a restlessness would appear about then, owing to the (it seems) inevitable boredom of Men with the good: there would be secret societies practising dark cults, and 'orc-cults' among adolescents.)"

As he said in yet another letter:

“…for or course the Shadow will arise again in a sense (as is clearly foretold by Gandalf), but never again…will an evil daemon be incarnate as a physical enemy; he will direct Men and all the complications of half-evils, and defective-goods, and the twilights of doubt as to sides, such situations as he most loves (you can see them already arising in the War of the Ring, which is by no means so clear cut an issue as some critics have averred): those will be and are our more difficult fate."

And so it is our fate!

replies.author: @Tallorian
replies.text:
@brickstonesonn9276 I can agree that mediocrity gets forgotten fast and mostly the high quality shows stay in the annals of history, so comparing 90s to today is somewhat biased.

But I also think that the sheer number of anime produced each year has grown dramatically compared to the 90s - so the amount of lazy, poorly-made, money-grabbing slop has also increased to become almost overwhelming. And it's not only that they're using some techniques to fasten production (like 3d-ish CGI), but also cutting corners to make deadlines or save money.

This tendency can certainly be observed within the same titles in recent seasons compared to how they began. I've mentioned Konosuba s3 already, where (despite writing staying somewhat on the same level) the drop in visual quality irked me so much I couldn't even finish it. Other examples are Demon Lord Retry s2 (it wasn't a visual candy to begin with, but got reduced to absolute trash), or Yuru Camp s3 (which isn't specifically ugly, but notably uses photo-realistic backgrounds instead of hand-drawn ones, and the characters changed their appearance a lot, losing their uniqueness, becoming more like a template with different haircut and color).

More often than not this drop coincides with switching studio in charge of anime, and even though many of the original staff often stay the same it's safe to argue that the decline is driven by managerial/budgetary decisions rather than free artistic choice.

In the end, I can't name any recent anime which I could put on the same visual quality level as my favorite titles of the 90s-early 00s. Vinland Saga s1 maybe? I think it was quite gorgeous, but it's 2019, and the worst drop occurred somewhere after '21-22. I should check out Spice and Wolf, maybe it's an exception to the rule, thanks for pointing out.

@Morten_Storvik   And you shouldn’t be obsessed with turning every situation into “who’s oppressed.”  The reason why is because that doesn’t apply to every situation.  Such as in this fictional setting.  

I will also say that there are situations too where the bad guys are the ones not in control and are the minority within an area.  And in that situation too you really shouldn’t root for the bad guys to gain power.  

Those are flaws within the idea of “I will root for whoever is not currently in power and has less than this other group.”  Bad idea.  

And again, if that’s how you operate then you have no values.  You stand for nothing.  Because you’re saying by such a position that no matter what a group’s values are “I will support them if they’re the ones currently not in charge.”

You should root for Rohan even though the land was (and still is) contested by others living nearby.  You should root for Rohan even if the wild men happen to be in a less advantageous position than Rohan.  (Which again Rohan is backed by Gondor, which is essentially a large, influential ally.  And Nerdrotic explains that Gondor in the original story was part of who aided them too at the end.)

The hero of the War of Rohirrim is Fréaláf Hildeson, the nephew of Helm Hammerhand. He wasn't expected to inherit because Helm had two sons, Haleth and Hama. FUNCTIONALLY, Sauron and the orcs serve as an inarguable enemy... there is no different POV, no shades of gray, no ambiguity.  
They are evil.  Period.
They are the outside enemy that forces people who don't like each other (Elves and Dwarves, for example) to work together... to overcome their differences, or set them aside.

This is one of the fundamental reasons that it's popular all over the world... Lord of the Rings, Independence Day, War of the Worlds, all the books and movies where "space aliens" attack and force the different nations to work together.
That's an optimism, idealism, hopeful...

Trying to "humanize" them instead makes it cynical, nihilistic.
To use one of their favorite terms... it subverts the entire tone... which is to say, it RUINS it.

replies.text:
I actually thought she didn't do much to really hate her anyways. Helm kept gassing her up as Ms. "You Can Rule the World". Whatever you say, big guy. But her most significant contribution is throwing an alley oop to Frealaf so he can look more badass when he comes. That's it. I guess the retreat to Helm's Deep too, but her fucking handler broke more of a sweat than her. All she did was take Helm into Helm's Deep, something the soldiers and her remaining brother could do as well.

If they really wanted a female perspective on Rohan at the time of Helm Hammerhand, Hera could've just easily been an actual shieldmaiden. You know, a soldier? Perhaps a dame (that's a female squire or knight) for Helm's retinue? And Helm and Frealaf would've been the main focus of the story, as is with the lore, with this random female soldier bearing witness to it all. A literal retelling of a legend from long ago, from the eyes of a normal person in Middle Earth.

That would be interesting, in my opinion. Having a regular knight live through these events, interact with the normal people of Edoras, trying to put up a tough and hopeful resolve whilst her fears and sorrows gnaw at her, and watch as their faith in the good of the world helps them survive another day, thanks to these legendary heroes that she knew personally. She could've had her own story adjacent to the lore of Helm's Deep, watching her soldiers and fellow citizens deal with the worsening situation, fighting the urge to drop their posts and flee for their lives, and if Rohan would even survive at all.

I'm on the side that the movie was... generic, to say the least. Inoffensive if viewed on its own and with no background with LOTR. But the potential was there.

replies.author: @ManicPixie-bb7wc
replies.text:
​@paolozago6123That old weebish definition of anime is getting outdated.

 What exactly does "produced" in Japan even mean anymore in 2024? That only Japanese nationals invested money into the project, and only Japanese animators animate it while sitting inside a animation studio in Japan?  

Almost all mainstream anime today  are multinational productions with foreign investment and many non-Japanese animators working on the project remotely via the Retas cloud.  Big fish Japanese studios also rely heavily on Korean studios to do the in-betweening because it is cheaper to get it done in Korea. 

An animation produced and animated purely in Japan by Japanese is becoming increasingly rare as more and more small studios close down. 

At this point in time, "anime" can be and probably should be considered more of a style/aesthetic than purely an animation produced in one particular country. 

Welcome to the Post-Internet world where national and cultural boundaries start to blur.

Éowyn was a *real* strong female character in the original films, and it is insane that they got her actress to narrate this anime, trying to draw a parallel between her and Hera, like Hera is anywhere near the same level as her. Éowyn earned her power and her respect through her blood sweat and tears. Her onscreen presence is not soon forgotten. Who is going to remember this generic anime redhead?

People complain there aren’t enough female characters in the stories… but all the ones that do appear are forces of nature - Galadriel (Cate Blanchett, not the other one), Arwen, Éowyn. These characters are literally and figuratively powerful, and perfectly portrayed by the actresses that played them.

We *don’t* need “girl bossing” because it is shallow and fake and demeaning. Girl bosses have plot armor and male incompetence because the writers can’t imagine women being capable to do hard work on their own without those things. They admire strong characters like Èowyn, but they can’t write those character themselves because these writers are shallow.

Because it was Jackson, I gave it the benefit of doubt, but the more I looked at Philippa Boyens, and even Fran and found all of the feminist push in the movies came from them, even as far back as the original trilogy. They were constantly pushing to expand the female roles and make them less as Tolkien wrote them, and more in line with modern expectations, which is why Arwen's more of a warrior elf, and Eowyn goes on a "ain't no man gonna tell me" arc. They wanted Arwen at Helm's Deep until it was leaked and received a bit of a fan backlash, so she was removed. Apparently she's still visible in some shots of the theatrical release.

In the originals the agenda it was just 'relatively' low-key, but you can see how they gained more ground with each movie. Alas, the modern Message has insidiously worked its way to New Zealand too.

author: @comicfan313
text:
the movie is definitely flawed but i think it is still enjoyable. and i definitely have to disagree with a lot of points you made. 
I think they managed it relatively well to establish why Hera wasn't mentioned. most of her achievements simply get overshadowed by the achievements of other people or because there was no one to tell that part of the story. 
the first part is defeating the Mûmakil (Ollifant) but she didn't do it on her own, it was the watcher. the only witnesses could be wulf and his guys, but a lot of them are dead at the end and normally they aren't the ones who tell the stories. it's the winners who tell them. 
when she warned the civilians that Helm had traitor in his rows, it wasn't much that could be saved since everything was already in progress. you could argue if this is a part where a story would be written about in the legendarium. 
the next part is at the hornburg. where the only notable action that influences the outcome is the calling for aid from the eagles. This indeed is weird that it wouldn't be told about. 
the last part where she directly fights with wulf is mostly overshadowed by the force who indeed saves everyone, not the one who stalls for time. so in this case it is Fréaláf who defeats the enemy.

the next point I have to hard disagree is the Girlboss trope. she is above average in her physical abilities, but it is in no means unrealistic if we believe that she has trained since she was little. and you mentioned she is a brilliant strategist? I didn't really see that portrayed in the film. 
about her achivements, the first Mûmakil is defeated by the watcher as already mentioned. the people fighting of wulf doesn't seem like a special feat since he is portrayed as weak. surviving the fall is indeed a bit of a stretch, but it isn't like she is the only character in any story who has a bit of plot armor.
Hera warns Helm, but at that point she doesn't bring out points why it isn't the best. and without the traitor it seems smarter for Helm to engage in a field and I would say this is the smarter move as this brings out more the strenghts of the cavalery. 
only later it seems that she realises that there is a traitor after she sees the same seal that she saw at Isengard. But that was just basic counting one and one together. nothing out of the ordinary. The reveal only changes in retrospect how her advise would be smarter, but with the given knowledge that it seemed like they didn't have before. 
why do the brothers seem at that point like idiots? I don't understand your point here.
how she killed the lord in the stable you already mentioned it was only with the help of her horse. It seemed like she couldn't overpower him on her own.
killing the second Mûmakil was because she just pulled the trigger of a big crossbow. literally everyone could have done it. 
can't argue about the next point about the horse of her brother since I don't remember the dialog where she spelled that out.
the next part is not understandable for me how does she "girlboss" the lords at the hornburg? and why is it out of the ordinary? if the lord got previously got the command to hold the hornburg for cases of emergencies it would be totally stupid to "pick up their swords" in a sense to get to aid in the battle of edoras. if you mean why they didn't shoot wulf after he killed the hostage, then i have no clue and it must have been incompetence indeed. but i still don't see how they were protected by Hera (at that point) since no ongoing battle was fought, just a siege.
next point, yes Hera killed the big orc, but it was only the finishing blow by throwing a spear. which seems reasonable when she has trained her whole life. and it was probably only possible because the orc was occupied by Helm.
I would say that the movie doesn't diminishes Helms last stand. and he also frightened wulfs people before with skirmishes which I think were nicely protrayed how that struck them with fear. and also helped to buy them time.

replies.text: @JustTTone It's not about cultural appropriation.  A part of what makes it anime is the Japanese culture contained within it.  You can copy the art style but that's it.  It's a cultural artifact just like how Baseball is distinctly American.  Cultural artifacts are objects, practices, or expressions that have historical, artistic, or social significance. They can be tangible, like sculptures or paintings, or intangible, like songs or stories. Cultural artifacts can provide insight into a culture's values, beliefs, and societal norms.  A man can wear a skirt but that doesn't make it a kilt.

author: @leverage2279
text:
I've just watched it and it was good enough.

 I don't know why people fuss in the comments about the girl-boss thing. 

Héra was a realistic girl because she hasn't really done any thing beyond the physical capabilities of women. And she was even not confident enough when Helm was leaving her to lead the people, she said "I can't." So she is believable enough. 

BUT the only thing that disappointed me severely in the movie is that there were 2 cavalry charges and only 5 or 10 riders were being seen! 

I mean the battle in the night when the king led the Rohirrim was not a mere scout skirmish. It was an army vs. arrm battle, and yet there were only 5 to 10 riders being seen on each scene, and we are left to try and imagine that they were thousands and thousands.

Also, in the end when Héra was cornered and Fréalaf and his riders came to save them in the 2nd cavalry charge in the movie, it was supposed to be at least hundreds upon hundreds of riders charging down on the Dunlendings--- BUT at each scene, there were only 5 or 10 riders being seen and we are again left to imagine that they were hundreds and hundreds.

Every fight scene seems like a skirmish scene. There were true skirmishes where only a few warriors were involved, but those 2 battles could not have involved 5 to 10 knights of Rohan! 

The night battle near  Edoras where the king hinself led, that wpuld have involved thousands of riders against thousands of Dunlendings! And we saw only a handful warriors like 5 or 10 at each scene.

In the siege when Fréalaf charged down, it was supposed to be hundreds of riders bearing down against thousands of Dunlendings, and yet again we saw only 10 or 15 at every fighting scene!

I can't help thinking: "Why do they have to shoot a close up scene at every fight? Why not take the camera well above the fight so that all the thousands of warriors could be seen!"

Still, nothing could beat those fight scenes in the Return of the King where you really feel the narration of Tolkien about the thousands of warriors in the battles, portrayed the way the should be!
